lesson.title: Saving & Compound Interest
lesson.explanation: Saving means setting money aside for future use. An emergency fund (3-6 months of expenses) protects against unexpected costs like car repairs or job loss. Compound interest is interest earned on both your principal and previously earned interest: A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt), where P is principal, r is annual rate, n is compounding frequency, and t is years. The Rule of 72 estimates doubling time: 72 / interest rate = years to double. Starting early matters enormously because compounding accelerates over time.

examples.problem: Zanele saves R5,000 in a savings account earning 7% annual interest, compounded monthly. How much will she have after 5 years?

steps.instruction: Identify the variables: P=5000, r=0.07, n=12, t=5

examples.answer: After 5 years, Zanele will have approximately R7,088 — earning R2,088 in interest on a R5,000 deposit.

examples.problem: How long will it take to double R10,000 at 8% annual interest using the Rule of 72?
examples.answer: It takes approximately 9 years to double your money at 8% interest.

examples.problem: Themba's monthly expenses are R12,000. How much does he need in an emergency fund (3-month minimum)?

steps.result: Saving R3,000/month reaches the minimum in 12 months

examples.answer: Themba needs R36,000 minimum (R72,000 ideal). At R3,000/month, he reaches the minimum in 1 year.

practice_problems.question: Using the Rule of 72, how long does it take to double your money at 6% interest?
practice_problems.explanation: 72 / 6 = 12 years. This is an approximation but remarkably accurate for rates between 2-15%.

practice_problems.question: Why does compound interest grow faster than simple interest?
practice_problems.answer: Compound interest earns interest on previously earned interest, not just the original principal. Each period, the base amount grows, so interest in later periods is larger.
hints: Think about what happens to the interest you earned last year
practice_problems.explanation: With simple interest, R1,000 at 10% always earns R100/year. With compound interest, year 1 earns R100, year 2 earns R110 (10% of R1,100), and so on.
distractors: It uses a higher interest rate
It is the same but calculated differently

practice_problems.question: If you invest R2,000 at 10% compounded annually, how much do you have after 3 years?
hints: Use A = P(1 + r)^t since compounding is annual (n=1)
practice_problems.explanation: A = 2000 x (1.10)^3 = 2000 x 1.331 = R2,662. You earned R662 in interest.

practice_problems.question: What is the recommended size of an emergency fund?
practice_problems.answer: 3 to 6 months of essential living expenses
hints: Think about how long it might take to find a new job
practice_problems.explanation: 3 months is the minimum safety net; 6 months provides more security, especially for those with dependents or variable income.

practice_problems.question: Person A starts saving R500/month at age 25. Person B starts saving R1,000/month at age 35. Both earn 8% annually and retire at 60. Who has more? Why?
practice_problems.answer: Person A has more (approximately R1,140,000 vs R920,000) despite saving half the monthly amount, because they had 10 extra years of compounding.
hints: Compound interest rewards time more than amount
practice_problems.explanation: Person A: 35 years of compounding on R500/month. Person B: 25 years on R1,000/month. The extra decade of growth for Person A outweighs Person B's doubled contribution.
distractors: Person B because they save more each month
They end up with the same amount
Person A only if interest rates are above 10%

key_vocabulary.definition: Interest calculated on both the initial principal and all previously accumulated interest
key_vocabulary.example_usage: Thanks to compound interest, my R10,000 grew to R19,672 in 10 years at 7%.

key_vocabulary.definition: Savings set aside specifically for unexpected expenses or income loss, typically 3-6 months of expenses
key_vocabulary.example_usage: My emergency fund covered the R8,000 car repair without going into debt.

key_vocabulary.definition: A quick formula to estimate how many years it takes to double an investment: divide 72 by the annual interest rate
key_vocabulary.example_usage: At 9% interest, the Rule of 72 says my money doubles in about 8 years.

key_vocabulary.definition: The original amount of money saved or invested, before any interest is earned
key_vocabulary.example_usage: I deposited a principal of R5,000 into my savings account.

misconceptions.misconception: I will start saving when I earn more
misconceptions.correction: The power of compound interest depends on time, not amount. Saving even R200/month starting today beats saving R1,000/month starting 10 years later.
misconceptions.why_students_think_this: People underestimate exponential growth and overestimate the impact of a larger future salary.

misconceptions.misconception: A savings account is the best place for all savings
misconceptions.correction: Savings accounts are ideal for emergency funds (accessible, low risk). For long-term growth, investments typically outperform savings account interest rates after inflation.
misconceptions.why_students_think_this: Savings accounts feel safe and familiar, and the distinction between short-term and long-term saving is not widely taught.

misconceptions.misconception: Compound interest only matters for large amounts
misconceptions.correction: Compounding works on any amount. R100/month at 10% for 30 years becomes over R200,000. The percentage growth is the same regardless of the starting amount.
misconceptions.why_students_think_this: Small numbers produce small absolute returns initially, making the growth seem negligible.
